raw_recent_text: "Ugh, not again," you spout as the old wagon shutters, then dies.
Stranded on the road again, but this time, your money has gone dry.
All but your 50 dollar bill that Grandma gave you on her last Christmas.
You've been saving it for when you needed it most, but surely, it won't be enough.
The door creaks open, and you step out into the night sky, coughing up the smoke from your fried vehicle.
After pushing your car off the road and between the trees, there isn't much else left for you to do,
so you begin to wander down the dark, lonely street.
But at the end of the road, where concrete turned to stone turned to dirt, you notice a light up ahead, on the top of a hill.
As you waltz into the old, wooden shack, your eyes begin to light up with the fire of a thousand suns.
Roulette wheels! Poker tables! And in a dark corner of the abandoned casino, sits a dealer, shuffling cards for a new round of Blackjack.
That 50 dollars might just come in handy after all. Thanks, Grandma!
As you go to sit down at the table, you hear the Dealer cough, then watch as he sits up.
In a deep, and yet strained voice, the Dealer, cloaked in darkness, poses a question to you.
Would you like to play a game of Blackjack?
You have $50

You slept so hard you forgot where you were for a second. That's the good kind of confusion.
A scraggly stray cat jumps onto your hood and stares at you through the windshield.
It meows. Loudly. Insistently. Like it's judging your entire existence.
You share your meager rations. The cat purrs and curls up on your hood.
It's still there when you wake up the next morning. You have a friend now.
You decide to call it Whiskers.
You have befriended Whiskers!
Whiskers has been grooming herself since before dawn. Looking sharp for another day of doing nothing.
Having Whiskers by your side makes the world a little less cruel.
As you're walking along the side of the road, something catches your eye. A flash of white, darting between the bushes. A rabbit!
Without thinking, you give chase. The little creature bounds ahead of you, zigging and zagging through the underbrush with seemingly effortless grace.
You run and run, but no matter how fast you go, the rabbit stays just out of reach. Its white tail bobs mockingly in the moonlight.
Finally, you stop, hands on your knees, gasping for breath. When you look up, the rabbit is gone, vanished into the night like it was never there.
You trudge back to your wagon, defeated. But something tells you this isn't the last time you'll see that rabbit.
As the sun begins to set, and the stars light up in the night sky, you walk to the casino, eager to play more Blackjack.
You smell like daylight and poor decisions. Sit.

If there's one thing I've learned, it's that you must never back down, and never give up.
This one's straight from the fortune cookie in my lunch:

"Every form has its own meaning. Every man creates his meaning and form and goal. Why is it so important - what others have done? Why does it become sacred by the mere fact of not being your own? Why is anyone and everyone right - so long as it's not yourself? Why does the number of those others take the place of truth? Why is truth made a mere matter of arithmetic - and only of addition at that? Why is everything twisted out of all sense to fit everything else? There must be some reason. I don't know. I've never known it. I'd like to understand."

Your body feels like it got a factory reset overnight. Joints? Smooth. Back? Fine. Mood? Immaculate.
Whiskers stares out the window.
Whiskers sits in a sunbeam, ignoring your existence with practiced elegance.
Your heart is pounding out of your chest. You can't breathe. You're dying - you're sure of it.
Except you're not. This is a panic attack. But it feels like death.
Trembling, sweating, derealization. The world doesn't feel real.
Severe anxiety disorder. You need medication. Therapy. Something.

You wake up to scratching noises. Something is ON your car.
You look up. A family of raccoons is making themselves at home on your roof.
Mom raccoon. Dad raccoon. Three baby raccoons. All staring at you through the windshield with those little bandit masks.
You honk the horn. They don't flinch. You honk again. The babies yawn.
You stare at each other for a full minute. They're not leaving. This is their car now.
You go back to sleep. In the morning, they've left a dead mouse on your hood. A gift? A threat? Both?
As the sun begins to set, and the stars light up in the night sky, you walk to the casino, eager to play more Blackjack.
The green felt remembers you. So do I.
You have $40

For those who enjoy a bit of bathroom philosophy:

"In the grand tapestry of existence, one must consider the intricate dance of the cosmos, where each celestial body moves in perfect harmony, except when they don't, which is most of the time, really, leading one to ponder whether the stars are just freestyling it. This brings to mind the importance of breakfast, the most important meal of a day that is itself a construct, much like the notion that socks should always match or that cats have any respect for personal space, which is to say, it's all a matter of perspective, isn't it? And speaking of perspective, have you ever noticed how small a plane looks in the sky, which is itself a vast canvas of blue, or gray, or black, depending on the time, which, as we've established, is a construct?"
Press a key to continue: ~ ~ ~ Morning, Day 5 ~ ~ ~  True
You wake up refreshed and optimistic. The optimism will probably wear off by noon, but for now, you bask in it.
Through your windshield, there's a car parked right in front of you. That wasn't there before.
Confused, and dazed, you sit up. As you open the door and get out of your car,
you notice a man, in a bright red suit, peering into your trunk.
The man sees you, and walks up to you.
"You. You're awake. Good. You know that you aren't supposed to be here? "
"This isn't a spot for people to live. This is a road for people to drive. I hope you know this."
"Do you know this?"
You wake up refreshed and optimistic. The optimism will probably wear off by noon, but for now, you bask in it.
Through your windshield, there's a car parked right in front of you. That wasn't there before.
Confused, and dazed, you sit up. As you open the door and get out of your car,
you notice a man, in a bright red suit, peering into your trunk.
The man sees you, and walks up to you.
"You. You're awake. Good. You know that you aren't supposed to be here? "
"This isn't a spot for people to live. This is a road for people to drive. I hope you know this."
"Do you know this?"
"You don't know this? How don't you know this? It's super obvious stuff, man. "
"People don't live at places where they're not supposed to, and that's exactly what you're doing right now. "
"I'd suggest you stop it, right this instant."
After the man tells you this, he looks up, and stares at the sun. And after about 20 seconds, he rubs his eyes, walks back to his car, and drives off.
Whiskers swipes at your hand when you try to pet her. No claws. But the message is clear.
You lean against the hood of the wagon and look up. You find Orion's Belt. The Big Dipper. That's... all you know.
But it doesn't matter. The names don't matter. What matters is that something enormous and ancient is looking back at you.
And it doesn't care about your problems. Somehow, that's comforting.
Still, it's beautiful. You feel small, but in a comforting way.
As the sun begins to set, and the stars light up in the night sky, you walk to the casino, eager to play more Blackjack.
Back for more? The cards appreciate your persistence.

Your eyes pop open and you're ready to go. Did someone slip you a good dream? Because that was chef's kiss.
A seagull dive-bombs your car
and steals your breakfast right out of your hand.
MINE! MINE! MINE!
You chase it for half a block before giving up. Seagulls are nature's assholes.
Whiskers hisses at you when you reach for her. Okay. Message received.
Three AM. Four AM. Five AM. You've watched every hour tick by on the dashboard clock.
Your body is exhausted. Your brain is throwing a party. An awful party where every thought is about what you did wrong today.
At 5:47 AM, you finally drift off. Your alarm goes off at 6:00.
As the sun begins to set, and the stars light up in the night sky, you walk to the casino, eager to play more Blackjack.
Come, sit down, we have a game to play.

A man with a shotgun. Ski mask. Shaking hands. This is really happening.
The cashier freezes. A mother clutches her child. An old man drops his coffee.
The robber swings the gun around, wild-eyed.
You make it. Hide behind boxes of toilet paper and canned goods.
You can hear everything. The shouting. The crying. The crash of the register hitting the floor.
Then sirens. Running footsteps. Silence.
The police find you an hour later, still hiding. Curled up like a child.
Your mind feels... foggy. Something isn't right.
Whiskers won't eat. She just stares at the food, then at you, then turns away.

You sit across from yourself and lose every hand. You can't beat yourself. You never could.
You wake up and avoid the mirror for the rest of the morning.

"Howdy, partner! The name's Jameson. Davey Jameson.
"I happen to notice you were admiring my steed. He's a beauty, isn't he. "
"You see, it's common courtesy when a cowboy rides by, "
to give their mighty steed a carrot, as a way to express your gratitude for their hardwork and commitment to the job."
"You my friend, you are carrotless. That's quite a disrespectful showing towards my steed. "
"My, my, this can't do at all. What if another cowboy comes by, you're just gonna disrespect their steed, too? "
"Tell you what, I happen to have one spare carrot in my pouch. Take this, and be ready. "
"You never know when a cowboy's gonna come trotting by."
Davey Jameson hands you his Carrot, and smiles.
"See, with this carrot in your possession, you're ready for anytime a cowboy strolls on down this road. "
"Just give their steed a carrot, and they'll be very grateful."
And with that, Jameson reins his horse high into the air, gives you a yee-haw, then dashes off down the road.
Whiskers knocks your coffee off the dashboard. On purpose. While maintaining eye contact.
Rain on the roof. Not a storm — just rain. The soft kind. The kind that taps a lullaby on the metal above you.
For the first time in weeks, your brain shuts up. No calculations. No strategies. No what-ifs.
Just you, the rain, and eight uninterrupted hours.
You wake up and for three seconds — three beautiful seconds — you forget where you are.
As the sun begins to set, and the stars light up in the night sky, you walk to the casino, eager to play more Blackjack.
Take your seat. The wheel of fortune turns for no one.

You slept like a baby. A big, grown baby in a car, but a baby nonetheless.
You took a wrong turn. Or maybe the road took it for you.
You end up at a cemetery. Old one. Iron fence, stone angels, headstones so weathered
the names have worn to whispers.
Your car dies at the gate. Engine just... stops. You turn the key. Nothing.
You're stuck here until it decides to work again.
"Baby James, 2008-2008. Too good for this world."
That last one stops you. You stand there for a while.
You hear digging. Somewhere deeper in the cemetery, someone is working.
Whiskers is grooming excessively. Stress cleaning. She's not okay.
Sitting in your car, your mind drifts. You dream of water. Dark water. Deep water. Rising water.
You're in a car—your car—and water is pouring in through every crack.
You can't open the doors. The pressure is too much. The windows won't break.
The water reaches your waist. Your chest. Your neck. Your mouth.
You scream but water fills your lungs instead of air.
You wake up GASPING. Clawing at your throat. Soaking wet with sweat.
Just a dream. Just a dream. Just a—
Your feet are wet. You look down.
Rain. It's raining. Water leaked through a crack in your window.
Just rain. You're fine. You're fine. You're fine.
Your mind feels... foggy. Something isn't right.
As the sun begins to set, and the stars light up in the night sky, you walk to the casino, eager to play more Blackjack.
You feel anxious tonight.

Through the windshield, a car is parked right in front of you again.
Tired and concerned, you sit up. As you open the door and step out,
you notice a man you've met before, in his bright red suit, once again peering into your trunk.
The man sees you, and walks up to you, with a clipboard in his hand.
"You. You're awake. Good. You see this clipboard? It says you can't be here."
You begin to read the paper on the clipboard. It's a message, written in Comic Sans.
It reads 'This offical message from the government and the military and the army says that you can't be here.
That's right, you, the person reading this message right now, living on this land right here.
It's not for you. It won't ever be for you. So, you can't live here. You need to move right now, or I'll be very very angry.'
"Did you read it?"

You wake up with energy. Like, real energy. Not 'I didn't die in my sleep' energy.
You hear the familiar sound of sneakers scraping against concrete, and the rhythmic
slapping of a jump rope. It's Suzy.
"Hey, ""1! Remember me? It's Suzy!"
"I have a very important question for you today. VERY important."
"What's your favorite color?"
She stares at you with the intensity of someone who will remember this answer forever.
Tell her your favorite color:
"1?! That's a GREAT color! I love 1!"
"I'm going to remember that forever. You like 1. Got it."
Suzy writes something in a tiny notebook she pulls from her pocket,
nods seriously, then continues jump roping down the road.
You dream that you've been playing for a hundred years. Your beard drags on the ground. Your clothes are dust.
The Dealer hasn't aged at all. He still smiles that smile. He'll be here forever.
"One more hand?" he asks. You nod. What else would you do?
You wake up. Check the mirror. No beard. But your eyes look a hundred years old.
As the sun begins to set, and the stars light up in the night sky, you walk to the casino, eager to play more Blackjack.
The casino lights seem too bright. Too hungry. They're watching you.
Another gambler. Another story waiting to unfold.

That was some quality sleep. Five stars. Would sleep again.
You count your money three times. You get a different number each time.
Nothing. Not a sound. Not a click. Not a whimper. The car is dead and it's your fault.
You sit there for a minute, key in the ignition, staring at the dashboard like it betrayed you. It didn't. You betrayed it.
You stand on the roadside, holding imaginary cables, hoping someone takes pity on you.
It takes two hours. TWO. You stand there like a scarecrow holding a sign that says "PLEASE." Finally, a minivan stops.
Rain on the roof. Not a storm — just rain. The soft kind. The kind that taps a lullaby on the metal above you.
For the first time in weeks, your brain shuts up. No calculations. No strategies. No what-ifs.
Just you, the rain, and eight uninterrupted hours.
You wake up and for three seconds — three beautiful seconds — you forget where you are.
As the sun begins to set, and the stars light up in the night sky, you walk to the casino, eager to play more Blackjack.
The casino lights seem too bright. Too hungry. They're watching you.
Back again? Let's get this show on the road.

Your current sanity: 35%
Your neck doesn't hurt. Your back doesn't hurt. Nothing hurts. Is this a trap?
You see them from the road. Lights. Dozens of them. Colored bulbs strung on wires,
ferris wheel silhouette against the night sky, the distant sound of calliope music.
A carnival. In the middle of nowhere. On a Tuesday.
There's a hand-painted sign at the entrance: "PROFESSOR MIDNIGHT'S TRAVELING SPECTACULAR"
Underneath, in smaller letters: "No refunds. No complaints. No escape."
That last one is probably a joke.
That last one is probably a joke.
The gates are open. No ticket booth. No admission fee. Just an archway of lights
and the smell of cotton candy and engine grease.
Inside, the carnival is alive. But something's off. The rides are running but nobody's
on them. The game booths are staffed but the attendants don't blink.
The cotton candy machine is spinning by itself.
A man in a top hat and a coat covered in patches approaches you. His smile is too wide.
"WELCOME, WELCOME! A new guest! How delightful! ""I am Professor Midnight, and everything you see here is REAL. ""The fun, the prizes, the danger — all REAL."
He gestures grandly at the carnival.
"Look around! Play a game! Visit the fortune teller! ""But do it quickly — we leave at dawn. We always leave at dawn."
He tips his hat and walks backward into the crowd, never turning around,
never breaking eye contact, and then he's gone.
In the dream, you walk into a bank. A real bank. With your name on the door.
The teller asks how much you'd like to deposit. You say "All of it." She doesn't blink.
You sign papers. Buy a house. A car that isn't also your bedroom. You eat at a restaurant with cloth napkins.
Then you wake up. The steering wheel is digging into your ribs. But the smile lingers.
As the sun begins to set, and the stars light up in the night sky, you walk to the casino, eager to play more Blackjack.
The casino lights seem too bright. Too hungry. They're watching you.
Are you ready to play some Blackjack?

You wake up smiling. When's the last time that happened? You can't remember, but it happened today.
You hear a knock on your window. Cop knock. The heavy, authoritative kind.
You sit up and see a police officer standing outside your car. He's stocky, late 40s,

"License and registration, please."
You fumble around. You don't actually have a registration. You're not even sure where your license is.
You decide to let them have this one. You go back to sleep.

Raccoons. Nature's tiny, adorable home invaders.

You slept so hard you forgot where you were for a second. That's the good kind of confusion.

It's playing your least favorite song. Of course it is.
You turn it off in disgust.
Your knee has seen better days. Much, much better days.
After wandering from your vehicle, you find yourself deep in the woods.
Squirrels run by and up into the trees. The sun hits every branch and casts a shadow below.
And you wander on a natural path, journeying into the unknown.
As you walk along the path, you notice someone leaning against a tree in front of you.
As you get closer, you notice that the person's face is blue, their eyes are bloodshot, and they don't appear to be breathing.
You begin to panic, before thinking through the situation.
They're already dead, so there's nothing you can do to help them.
Maybe they had some money on them? I mean, they're not gonna use it. Why shouldn't you?
Do you search the body?
You rummage through the pockets, trying to find anything worthwhile.
After a minute of digging, you manage to find a wallet. Score!
Inside the wallet, you find $125 dollars.
Your new balance is $150 + $125 = $275
You leave the dead body, and continue down the path,
until the forest opens up to the main road. You follow the road back to your wagon, with your winnings in hand.

You wake up and actually want to get out of the car. This is a historic moment.
You come back to your car and find a cream-colored business card tucked beneath the wiper blade.
The paper is thick, expensive, and somehow greasy at the same time.
On the front, embossed in gold, it reads: Vinnie's Back Alley Loans
On the back is a handwritten note: "If the banks don't believe in you, I might. Come alone."
You glance up and notice a black sedan idling at the far end of the lot. The driver gives you a two-finger salute and pulls away before you can get a plate.
The whole thing feels like a terrible idea. Which probably means it'll be useful someday.

After wandering from your vehicle, you find yourself deep in the woods.
Squirrels run by and up into the trees. The sun hits every branch and casts a shadow below.
And you wander on a natural path, journeying into the unknown.
As you walk along the path, you find a mother deer, with two children, walking the path towards you.
As you get closer, the mother appears cautious, but then runs in your direction, before stopping before you.
Her two children follow behind, and before you know it, the three of them wait in front of you.
You put your hand out, and pet the mother deer. She makes a happy squeak noise, and wags her tail.
She touches her head to yours, then continues down the path, with her two children following.
Eventually, you get to the end of the path, and find the main road. You follow it back to your wagon, and take a seat, to rest for a moment.
As the sun begins to set, and the stars light up in the night sky, you drive over to the casino, eager to play more Blackjack.
The neon signs blur together. For a moment, you can't remember why you came here.
The casino never sleeps. Neither do you, it seems.
The Dealer looks at you strangely. His jade eye narrows.
He does NOT seem pleased to see you tonight.

You stretch out and feel every vertebra in your spine crack in the most satisfying way. Today is going to be good.
You feel unstable.
You feel unstable.
A car pulls up next to you. Windows tinted black. Engine rumbling.
The window rolls down. A face stares out. Cold eyes. Gold teeth.
"You the one been asking around? Looking for... product?"
You haven't been asking around. This is a case of mistaken identity.
"I think you've got the wrong guy. I don't—"
He cuts you off. "Don't play dumb. Marcus said you was looking."
You don't know any Marcus. Your heart is pounding.
He doesn't believe you. Door opens. He gets out. He's holding something metal.
"You talked to the cops, didn't you? You a snitch?"
Before you can answer, his fist connects with your face. Then again. Then the metal thing—a pipe.
You go down. They beat you until you stop moving. Take your wallet. Leave you bleeding.
Your new balance is $19,544 - $314 = $19,230

The radio turns on by itself. It plays a song that doesn't exist.
You wade waist-deep through the swamp, the water cold and thick with silt.
Every step is a struggle, and unseen things brush against your legs.
The air is heavy with the scent of decay and blooming lilies.
Fireflies blink in the darkness like tiny green stars.
You nearly trip over something beneath the surface - the edge of a sunken rowboat, half-buried in the muck.
Do you try to pull it up and search it?
You wrestle the rotting boat to the surface. Inside, tangled in algae and crawfish, you find...
Nothing but rot, mud, and a family of very angry crawfish. One pinches your finger hard enough to draw blood.
As the sun begins to set, and the stars light up in the night sky, you drive over to the casino, eager to play more Blackjack.
The neon signs blur together. For a moment, you can't remember why you came here.
The cards have been waiting for you.
The Dealer looks at you strangely. His jade eye narrows.
He does NOT seem pleased to see you tonight.

your sanity: broken
your reflection in the cards is smiling. you're not.
you had a dream about winning at blackjack. then you woke up. but you still feel great.
a glossy black limousine is quietly approaching your wagon.
as you sit up from your slumber, you read 'oswald's optimal outoparts' cursively engraved in gold letters on the side of the limo.
waving the vehicle down, the limo slows, then stops before you.
the door opens vertically, and a large red carpet is rolled out onto the street.
you watch in awe as a man, with a combover and a tuxedo, walks out before you. he coughs, then speaks.
"why hello there! the name's oswald, as you can see by my nametag.
do you like my bowtie? well of course you do! it appears your limousine has broken down."
oswald pulls a gold whistle out of his pocket, and blows into it deeply.
"oh stuart!" you watch as a bald man in a tailcoat suit, no taller than 4 feet, hobbles over to oswald's side.
"this is stuart! he will fix your limousine up for a fair price.
let's say, i don't know, i suppose a fair price is 500,000 dollars.
okay, the look on your face says that i'm making a big mistake.
let's try $800, and stuart here will get you back on the road! do you accept?"
"really? you don't want my services? i'm so sorry stuart, but it appears they don't want our services."
stuart begins to break down into tears, and he runs quickly back into the limo.
"shame on you! shame on you! i hope to never see the likes of you again."
you watch as oswald rolls up the red carpet, gets back in the limo, and drives off into the distance.

after playing a few rounds of blackjack, the dealer points to the door.
without questioning his word, and with your winnings in hand, you scurry to the door, eager to get some sleep.
you make it to your car and drive away from the casino,
and you park in a little alcove on the side of the road.
you lay down, and close your eyes. it's time to rest.
you drift into a dream, and find yourself sitting at a bar inside a grand casino.
crystal chandeliers hang from the ceiling, their light dancing across marble floors.
the air smells of expensive cigars and possibility.
you're chatting it up with the person next to you-someone important, though you can't quite remember who. a bartender in a crisp white shirt approaches.
"what'll it be?"
you order a drink. the bartender raises an eyebrow.
"that's gonna cost you. expensive taste you've got."
you wave your hand dismissively. money is no object. not here. not in this place.
the bartender shrugs and pours your drink. as he slides it across the bar, he says:
"well, it's your drink after all."
you wake up, the phantom taste of bourbon lingering on your tongue.
you can almost hear the slot machines in the distance.

your reflection in the cards is smiling. you're not.
you had a dream about winning at blackjack. then you woke up. but you still feel great.
a glossy black limousine is quietly approaching your wagon.
as you sit up from your slumber, you read 'oswald's optimal outoparts' cursively engraved in gold letters on the side of the limo.
waving the vehicle down, the limo slows, then stops before you.
the door opens vertically, and a large red carpet is rolled out onto the street.
you watch in awe as a man, with a combover and a tuxedo, walks out before you. he coughs, then speaks.
"why hello there! the name's oswald, as you can see by my nametag.
do you like my bowtie? well of course you do! it appears your limousine has broken down."
oswald pulls a gold whistle out of his pocket, and blows into it deeply.
"oh stuart!" you watch as a bald man in a tailcoat suit, no taller than 4 feet, hobbles over to oswald's side.
"this is stuart! he will fix your limousine up for a fair price.
let's say, i don't know, i suppose a fair price is 500,000 dollars.
okay, the look on your face says that i'm making a big mistake.
let's try $800, and stuart here will get you back on the road! do you accept?"
"really? you don't want my services? i'm so sorry stuart, but it appears they don't want our services."
stuart begins to break down into tears, and he runs quickly back into the limo.
"shame on you! shame on you! i hope to never see the likes of you again."
you watch as oswald rolls up the red carpet, gets back in the limo, and drives off into the distance.
